name: lahamu (silt)
shortDescription: Lachmu and Lahamu are primordial deities in Babylonian mythology, representing silt and mud. They are the first pair of gods to emerge from the mingling of Apsu and Tiamat.
description: Lahamu and her brother-husband Lachmu are primordial deities in Babylonian mythology, representing the silt and mud that emerged from the mingling of Apsu (fresh water) and Tiamat (salt water). They are the first pair of gods to be born after Apsu and Tiamat united, representing the solid ground and foundation upon which the later cosmos would be built. As offspring of the primordial waters, they are integral to the Babylonian creation myth, acting as a bridge between the formless chaos and the structured world. While not actively involved in later myths, their existence is crucial as the progenitors of the younger gods and the tangible representation of the first separation within the primordial waters. They are often depicted as serpentine or dragon-like figures, reflecting their connection to the watery abyss and their primordial nature. Their names are etymologically linked to the words for 'mud' and 'silt,' further emphasizing their role as personifications of the earliest geological formations.

sources.title: Myths from Mesopotamia: Creation, The Flood, Gilgamesh and Others
sources.author: Stephanie Dalley

sources.title: Before Philosophy
sources.author: Henri Frankfort, H.A. Frankfort, John A. Wilson, Thorkild Jacobsen
